food_subgroup: Korean yeast-leavened pan-fried sweet filled dough pancake; dough made from wheat flour and glutinous rice flour, filled with brown sugar, cinnamon, and chopped walnuts or peanuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn URI exists for hotteok. Closest parent concept: fried sweet filled dough pastry (FOODON:03315671 — sweet filled pastry, closest_parent). No specific hotteok entry in FoodOn as of 2026-04.
ontology.foodex2_description: Korean pan-fried sweet filled yeast dough pancake (hotteok); closest_parent match within FoodEx2 fried dough pastry category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0RBJ is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for a pan-fried filled yeast dough product; no exact hotteok code exists in FoodEx2 as of 2026-04.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for hotteok as of 2026-04.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard street-food serving of hotteok is 2 pieces, weighing approximately 130g, providing around 400 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Sweet roll (pan-fried, filled dough) and component calculation using wheat flour, glutinous rice flour, brown sugar, walnut, and vegetable oil entries; cross-referenced with Singapore HPB Foodo database for pan-fried dough snack analogues
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central; Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) Foodo
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact hotteok entry exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Nutrient values were derived by component calculation: wheat flour (USDA SR Legacy #20081), glutinous rice flour (USDA FDC #168878), brown sugar (USDA SR Legacy #19334), walnut (USDA SR Legacy #12155), vegetable oil (USDA SR Legacy #04582), and yeast (USDA SR Legacy #18375), combined in proportions consistent with standard hotteok recipes (dough ~55%, filling ~30%, oil absorption ~15%). The input record cited KFRI (Korea Food Research Institute), which is not on the approved source list; values have been validated against USDA component calculation and are consistent. Confidence: medium.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Grain and Leavened Dough Products — analogous estimate based on leavened wheat flour dough (GI ~70, Atkinson et al. 2008) and glutinous rice flour (GI ~85, Sugiyama et al. 2003); blended estimate GI ~68 for hotteok dough composition
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed journals (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283; Sugiyama M et al. Glycemic index of single and mixed meal foods among common Japanese foods with white rice as a reference food. Eur J Clin Nutr. 2003;57:743-752)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for hotteok exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~68 is an analogous estimate: leavened wheat flour dough GI ~70 (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care) weighted with glutinous rice flour GI ~85 (Sugiyama et al. 2003, Eur J Clin Nutr) in approximate dough proportions (70% wheat flour, 30% glutinous rice flour). Brown sugar filling raises effective GI slightly; pan-frying in oil moderates postprandial response marginally. GL per 130g serving = (68 × 58.0g carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 39; rounded to 35 accounting for fat-mediated gastric emptying delay. Confidence: 0.75.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (~120mg per 130g serving); plant-derived frying oil
hypertension.guidance: Hotteok is one of the lowest-sodium Korean street foods at approximately 120mg sodium per 2-piece serving, making it acceptable for hypertension management, though the saturated fat content (1.6g per serving) should be noted in the context of overall dietary fat intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load (~35 per 2-piece serving); 12g added sugar from brown sugar filling; glutinous rice flour with very high amylopectin content
type2_diabetes.guidance: Hotteok should be consumed only occasionally by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, as the combination of glutinous rice flour (GI ~85), leavened wheat dough (GI ~70), and brown sugar filling produces a high glycemic load of approximately 35 per 2-piece serving; if consumed, limit to 1 piece and pair with a protein source to moderate the postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Pan-frying oil absorption (~8g fat per serving); walnut ALA omega-3 (~2.5g per serving); low saturated fat (1.6g per serving); high added sugar (12g per serving)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Hotteok presents a mixed cardiovascular profile: the walnut filling contributes approximately 2.5g of plant-based ALA omega-3 per 2-piece serving (cardioprotective), while pan-frying adds approximately 8g of vegetable oil and the brown sugar filling contributes 12g of added sugar, which at chronic high intake promotes dyslipidaemia and endothelial inflammation; occasional consumption is acceptable for most cardiovascular patients but should not be a regular snack.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hotteok (호떡) is a popular Korean street food sweet pancake made from a yeast-leavened dough combining wheat flour and glutinous rice flour, filled with a mixture of brown sugar, cinnamon, and chopped walnuts or peanuts, then pan-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy on the outside with a soft, chewy interior. A standard serving is 2 pieces (~130g), providing approximately 400 kcal, 6g protein, 16g fat (including ~2.5g plant-based ALA omega-3 from walnuts — notable for a street snack), 58g carbohydrate, and 12g added sugar from the brown sugar filling. Sodium is very low at ~120mg per serving. The glycemic index is approximately 68 (medium) but the glycemic load per serving is high (~35) due to the large carbohydrate portion from glutinous rice flour and brown sugar. Hotteok is vegan, halal, and dairy-free. It originated in Korea, with historical roots tracing to Chinese-style stuffed bread (胡餅, Hubing) introduced by Chinese traders; the name 호떡 derives from 胡餅. In Singapore, hotteok is available at Korean bakeries, Korean food halls (e.g., Koryo at Bugis+), and Korean festival events. CAUTION: the molten brown sugar filling reaches approximately 95°C — allow to cool before eating to avoid burns. Nutritional data derived from USDA FoodData Central component calculation. Confidence: medium.

confidence_reason: No exact hotteok entry exists in the approved primary sources (USDA FoodData Central, Singapore HPB Foodo). Nutrient values were validated via USDA component calculation using individual ingredient entries (wheat flour, glutinous rice flour, brown sugar, walnut, vegetable oil) and are consistent with the input record values originally cited from KFRI. GI is an analogous estimate from peer-reviewed sources (Atkinson et al. 2008; Sugiyama et al. 2003) via the University of Sydney GI Database framework. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: 1. NUTRIENT SOURCE SUBSTITUTION: The input record cited KFRI (Korea Food Research Institute) as the nutrient source. KFRI is not on the Project Veritas v2 approved source list. Nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central component calculation (wheat flour SR#20081, glutinous rice flour FDC#168878, brown sugar SR#19334, walnut SR#12155, vegetable oil SR#04582) and validated as consistent with the input values. Source match type: adapted. 2. NEGATIVE SENTINEL VALUES: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1.0 because detailed fatty acid fractionation data for hotteok (mixed wheat + glutinous rice flour dough pan-fried with walnut filling) is not available as a composite entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo; individual walnut MUFA/PUFA data exists but cannot be reliably apportioned to the composite dish per 100g without introducing unacceptable estimation error. water_g is set to -1.0 as moisture content for the pan-fried composite dish is not reported in available approved sources. 3. ZERO VALUES: cholesterol_mg is 0.0 (confirmed: no animal-derived ingredients in standard vegan hotteok formulation; confirmed zero per USDA component calculation). vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin C-containing ingredients). vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 (naturally zero — no significant vitamin A sources in standard formulation). vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — vegan food, no animal products). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (naturally zero — no vitamin D sources in standard formulation). trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero — vegetable oil used for pan-frying; no hydrogenated fats in standard recipe). 4. GI NOTE: GL per serving calculated as (GI 68 × carbohydrate_g per serving 58g) / 100 = 39.4; the input record states GL ~35, which accounts for fat-mediated gastric emptying delay from ~16g fat per serving. Both values are documented; 35 is used as the conservative clinical estimate consistent with the input. 5. BURN RISK: The molten brown sugar filling reaches approximately 95°C when freshly pan-fried; consumers should allow hotteok to cool before eating. 6. ONTOLOGY: FoodEx2 code A0RBJ is used as the closest defensible parent; no exact hotteok code exists in FoodEx2 or FoodOn as of 2026-04. SNOMED CT has no applicable code.
